coredata.dc:title: A novel electrochemical chiral interface based on the synergistic effect of polysaccharides for the recognition of tyrosine enantiomers
coredata.dc:creator: Zou, Jiao
Chen, Xiao-Qing
Zhao, Guo-Qing
Jiang, Xin-Yu
Jiao, Fei-Peng
Yu, Jin-Gang
coredata.dc:description:
Electrochemical chiral interface based on two polysaccharides, soluble starch (SS) and chitosan (CS), was fabricated and used for chiral recognition of tyrosine (Tyr) enantiomers via square wave voltammetry (SWV). The SS-CS composite was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. Under the optimized experimental conditions, the oxidation peak current ratio of L-Tyr to D-Tyr (IL/ID) and the difference between the peak potential (ΔEp = ED - EL) were observed to be 1.38 and 12 mV at the SS-CS/GCE. In addition, a good linear relationship between Tyr enantiomer concentration and peak current could be observed. The SS-CS/GCE exhibited good coefficients of determination (R
2
D-Tyr = 0.99631 and R
2
L-Tyr = 0.98333) for D-Tyr and L-Tyr in the concentration range 0.01–1.00 mM. The novel SS-CS/GCE showed an ability to predict the ratios of L- and D-Tyr in a racemic mixture and exhibited the possibility of qualitative and quantitative determination of Tyr enantiomers. The proposed SS-CS/GCE-based chiral sensor exhibited high anti-interference ability in the presence of 10-fold higher concentrations of physiological substances and various metal compounds. Meanwhile, the SS-CS/GCE possessed good repeatability and excellent reproducibility.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral electrochemical sensor
Polysaccharide
Soluble starch
Chitosan
Tyrosine enantiomers
Square wave voltammetry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical properties of the modified electrodes
Selectively chiral recognition of Tyr enantiomers
Effects of amount of coated SS-CS
Electrochemical chiral detection of Tyr enantiomers
Anti‑interference, reproducibility and stability of SS-CS/GCE
Chiral recognition of Tyr enantiomers in racemic solutions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the samples: (A) FE-SEM image of SS-CS/GCE; (B) FE-SEM image of SS-CS composite; (C) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of the samples: SS (a), CS (b) and SS-CS composite (c).

Electrochemical behaviors of the electrodes: (A) CV of different electrodes in 1 mM of K3Fe(CN)6 solution (containing 0.1 M of KCl) at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1; (B) Nyquist plots of different electrodes in 1 mM K3Fe(CN)6 solution (containing 0.1 M KCl) at open-circuit potential conditions (AC frequency range: 0.01 Hz to 10 kHz; AC amplitude: 5.0 mV); (C) CV of the SS-CS/GCE in 1 mM K3Fe(CN)6 solution (containing 0.1 M KCl) redox peak currents at different scan rates (10, 20, 50, 80, 100, 150 and 200 mV s−1). (D) Plots of the SS-CS/GCE redox peak currents as a function of scan rates. (E) SWV of different electrodes in 1 mM of L-Tyr solution (0.1 M of PB, pH 7.0).
SWV curve of 1 mM (a) L -Tyr and (b) D-Tyr at the electrodes: (A) bare GCE; (B) SS/GCE; (C) CS/GCE; (D) SS-CS/GCE.
Effects of amount of coated SS-CS composite (4, 5, 6, 7, 8 and 9 μL) for Tyr enantiomers (1 mM), (Inset: SWVs of (a) L-Tyr and (b) D-Tyr with different amount of coated SS-CS, respectively.).
Effects of quite time for Tyr enantiomers peak current at the SS-CS/GCE. (Inset: SWVs of (a) L-Tyr and (b) D-Tyr with different quite time, respectively.).

Effects of pH of PB solutions: (A) Plots of peak current versus Tyr enantiomers at different pH (5, 6, 7, 8 and 9). (Inset: SWVs of (a) L-Tyr and (b) D-Tyr with different pH on the SS-CS/GCE, respectively.). (B) The variation of the L-Tyr and D-Tyr of potentials versus pH values.
Effects of temperature on the recognition efficiency (IL/ID and ΔEp) of SS-CS/GCE toward Tyr enantiomers.
The calibration plots of peak currents versus Tyr concentrations at SS-CS/GCE. (Inset: SWVs of (a) L-Tyr and (b) D-Tyr with different concentrations, respectively.).

The amperometric response current ratios for 0.1 mM Tyr enantiomer with 1 mM of various potential interfering agents (AA, Glc, D-Phe, L-Glu, L-Lys, L-Thr, L-Cys, Pd(NO3)2, NaCl, CaCl2, Ni(NO3)2, Mg(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3, CuSO4, FeCl3, K2Cr2O7, Zn(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2, and KCl) at SS-CS/GCE in 0.1 M PB (pH 7.0): (A) L-Tyr and (B) D-Tyr.
The repeatability of SS-CS/GCE in 0.05 mM of L-Tyr or D-Tyr (containing 0.1 mM PB, pH 7.0).
Electrochemical performance of SS-CS/GCE towards D-Tyr in mixture solutions: (A) SWV curves; (B) Relationship between Ip and D-Tyr wt%; (C) Relationship between Ep and D-Tyr wt%.
The schematic diagram of the experimental procedure: (A) The preparation of SS-CS/GCE; (B) The proposed mechanism for the chiral electrochemical recognition of Tyr enantiomers on SS-CS/GCE.
A comparison of the developed sensors for the recognition of Tyr enantiomers.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: DPV: different potential voltammograms; L-Cys: L-cysteine; MGCE: magnetic glassy carbon electrode; MIP: molecular imprint; SL/SD: selectivity ratio; CyA: cysteic acid.

head.ce:title: A novel electrochemical chiral interface based on the synergistic effect of polysaccharides for the recognition of tyrosine enantiomers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety, Central South University, Changsha, Hunan 410083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety, Central South University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Electrochemical chiral interface based on two polysaccharides, soluble starch (SS) and chitosan (CS), was fabricated and used for chiral recognition of tyrosine (Tyr) enantiomers via square wave voltammetry (SWV). The SS-CS composite was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. Under the optimized experimental conditions, the oxidation peak current ratio of L-Tyr to D-Tyr (IL/ID) and the difference between the peak potential (ΔEp = ED - EL) were observed to be 1.38 and 12 mV at the SS-CS/GCE. In addition, a good linear relationship between Tyr enantiomer concentration and peak current could be observed. The SS-CS/GCE exhibited good coefficients of determination (R
2
D-Tyr = 0.99631 and R
2
L-Tyr = 0.98333) for D-Tyr and L-Tyr in the concentration range 0.01–1.00 mM. The novel SS-CS/GCE showed an ability to predict the ratios of L- and D-Tyr in a racemic mixture and exhibited the possibility of qualitative and quantitative determination of Tyr enantiomers. The proposed SS-CS/GCE-based chiral sensor exhibited high anti-interference ability in the presence of 10-fold higher concentrations of physiological substances and various metal compounds. Meanwhile, the SS-CS/GCE possessed good repeatability and excellent reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:para: Chirality is a widespread phenomenon in nature, which plays an outstanding role in the molecular/biomolecular level in the fields of biology, medicine, pharmaceutical science and biotechnology [1]. Most of bioactive substances are chiral molecules, such as amino acids, proteins, carbohydrates, enzymes and DNA [2]. Both enantiomers of chiral molecules have the same physical and chemical properties. However, they may vary greatly in biological interactions, pharmacological activities and metabolic behaviors for biological systems [3]. In general, only one chiral molecule may be effective in treating the disease and exhibit the desired characteristics during chiral recognition, while another chiral molecule may be ineffective or have serious side effects and even toxic effects [4]. Therefore, chiral recognition is of great significance in clinical and pharmaceutical applications [5].

Amino acids (AAs) could be used as important biomarkers for various metabolic diseases [6]. Tyrosine (Tyr) is an important precursor to various bio-signaling molecules such as epinephrine, norepinephrine and dopamine, all of which regulate mood. Tyr deficiency is associated with depression [7] and is also a risk factor for diabetes development [8]. Therefore, the construction of a chiral interface for chiral recognition of amino acids isomers is considered to be one of the most challenging tasks in the life sciences [9]. Currently, chiral discrimination of AAs enantiomers mainly include high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [11], colorimetric (CL) assay [12], fluorescence (FL) detection [13], molecular imprinting techniques (MIP) [14] and electrochemical methods [15,16], etc. The limitations of HPLC include its susceptibility to matrix interference, baseline drift and interference peaks. CE is not a highly sensitive and reproducible method, and it should be equipped with highly sensitive detectors and the samples need to be pretreated. CL usually provides low sensitivity, selectivity and anti-interference capacity [17–21]. FL is relatively more sensitive and more accurate for measuring AAs, but it is still not used to determine their enantiomers in racemic mixtures. As for MIP method, specific functional monomers, crosslinking agents and polymerization methods are always needed. Generally speaking, many of previously-reported technologies have shown some limitations such as expensive instrument, complicated sample preparation, time-consuming and extensive training requirements of operators, and their practical applications have been greatly hindered. Due to their advantages of lower cost, higher specificity, higher sensitivity, and faster response as well as easier to regenerate in analyte detection, various electrochemical approaches based on chiral selector modified electrodes have been developed for the enantioselective determination of AAs enantiomers [22]. A key step in the chiral electrochemical discrimination of AAs enantiomers is to construct a chiral surface with multiple recognition sites for distinguishing enantiomers [23]. The most common used chiral selectors are cyclodextrins (CDs) (α-CD [24], β-CD [25–27] and γ-CD [28]) and their derivatives, bovine serum albumin (BSA) [16,29], chitosan (CS) [24,30–32], calf thymus DNA [33], potato starch (PS) [34], human serum albumin (HSA) [35], AAs [36,37], sodium alginate (SA) [38] and so on. For example, amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots/β-CD composite of enriched with chiral sites (NH2-GQDs/β-CD/GCE) was developed for chiral selective detection of tryptophan (Trp) enantiomers [22]. The CS and Cu2+-coordinated α-CD (Cu2-α-CD) modified GCE (Cu2-α-CD-CS/GCE) could be used for electrochemical chiral recognition of Tyr enantiomers [24].

Composite materials have attracted growing attention in the electrochemical recognition/detection due to their specific functionality and high surface area [39–43]. Carbon materials (carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [15,44–46], graphene oxide (GO) [35,47], reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [28,48] and GQDs [25,49,50]), metal nanoparticles (AuNPs [29,48,51,52], PtNPs [53]), conductive polymers [54,55] and other nanomaterials possess high surface area, high intrinsic mobility and good electrical conductivity, which can enhance functional properties of sensors. The incorporation of nanomaterials and chiral selectors into the electrodes can significantly increase sensitivity of chiral sensors for electrochemical chiral recognition of enantiomers. For example, GQDs-CS/GCE was prepared via successive electrodeposition of GQDs and CS onto GCE, which showed higher chiral recognition efficiency for Trp enantiomers than that of any individual material modified electrode [49]. Other chiral materials including GQDs-β-CD [25], Cu2-β-CD [26] and MWCNTs-CS [56] were employed to modify electrodes for the electrochemical recognition of Trp enantiomers. Unfortunately, nanomaterials are expensive, environmentally unfriendly and difficult to recycle/reuse. Therefore, the development of simple, inexpensive, green and environmentally friendly materials for chiral recognition still remains an urgent task [57].

Natural polysaccharides such as soluble starch (SS) and CS are biocompatible, biodegradable and non-toxic natural polymers having natural chiral templates [58]. They showed better enantiomeric identification properties mainly because of multiple chiral recognition sites. At present, little attention has been paid to construct novel chiral interfaces based on different polysaccharides for identifying enantiomers. To the best of our knowledge, only one report by Kong et al. [30] introduced the fabrication of sodium carboxymethyl cellulose-chitosan (CMC-Na-CS) composite by amidation reaction between the carboxyl (-COOH) groups on CMC-Na and the amino (-NH2) groups on CS for the electrochemical recognition of Trp enantiomers. However, the process for preparation of CMC-Na-CS composite is relatively complex.

Herein, we developed a novel electrochemical sensor based on SS and CS modified glassy carbon electrode (SS-CS/GCE) for chiral recognition of Tyr enantiomers ( Scheme 1A) by square wave voltammetry (SWV). SS and CS were facilely integrated together by hydrogen bonding between the hydroxyl (-OH) groups on the SS and the -OH/-NH2 groups on the CS. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method were used to characterize SS-CS composite. The integrated SS-CS composite exhibited a porous network structure and improved hydrophilicity due to the exposed polar functional groups, making it an attractive chiral template for recognition of Tyr enantiomers. The chiral recognition mechanism might be attributed to the selective formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds between the Tyr enantiomers and SS-CS composite (Scheme 1B). The novel SS-CS/GCE fabricated in this study could be used as a convenient, fast and efficient chiral electrochemical sensor for the qualitative and quantitative determination of Tyr enantiomers.

L-Tyrosine ( L-Tyr, 99% purity) and D-Tyr (98% purity) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Chitosan (CS, B.R.; > 90.0% deacetylation degree) was obtained from Shanghai Zhanyun Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Starch soluble (SS), acetic acid (CH3COOH), disodium phosphate (Na2HPO4·12H2O) and sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4·2H2O) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Alumina polishing powder (0.05 µm) was bought from Lab Testing Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Potassium ferricyanide [K3Fe(CN)6] and potassium chloride (KCl) were purchased from Tianjin Broadcom Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China).

Phosphate buffer solution (PB; 0.1 M, pH = 7.0) was prepared using a certain amount of NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4. Electrochemical measurements were performed in a supporting electrolyte of PB solution. Stock solutions of D-Tyr and L-Tyr (1 mM) were prepared in 0.1 M of PB solution. The working solutions of D-Tyr and L-Tyr were prepared by diluting the stock solution with 0.1 M of PB solution. Each sample was measured three times consecutively to estimate the reproducibility, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated.

The morphologies of SS-CS/GCE and SS-CS composite were observed on a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, TESCAN MIRA3 LMH/LMU, Czech). The specific surface area of SS-CS composite was analyzed by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method on a surface area detecting instrument (Kubo X1000) analyzer. All electrochemical experiments were performed in a conventional three-electrode system on a CHI660E Electrochemical Workstation (Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd. Shanghai, China). The three-electrode system consists of a working electrode (bare GCE and modified electrodes), a Pt counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl reference electrode. All electrochemical experiments were performed at room temperature (25 °C) except for temperature control experiments. Error bars represent the standard deviations for three independent measurements.

SS was dispersed in water and heated to 130 °C for 30 min to form a homogeneous solution (2.0 mg mL−1), then the mixture was naturally cooled to room temperature for future use. 100 mg of CS was dissolved in 100 mL of 1.0% (v/v) acetic acid aqueous solution to obtain a 1.0 mg mL−1 CS solution, which was stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C before use. To 4 mL of SS solution was added 4 mL of CS solution, and the mixture was sonicated for 30 min to obtain a SS-CS composite.

Bare GCE (3 mm of diameter) was carefully polished with an alumina powder (0.05 µm) slurry and rinsed with double distilled water (DDW), then ultrasonically cleaned in DDW for 3 min. CV of the GCE in 1 mM K3Fe (CN)6 (containing 0.1 M KCl) solution within the potential range of 0–0.6 V (vs. AgCl) was carried out until the peak potential separation (ΔEp) was less than 80 mV. The GCE was rinsed with DDW and then dried under a flow of nitrogen. SS-CS/GCE was prepared by dropping different volumes of SS-CS solution on the GCE surface and then dried in air. CS and SS modified electrodes (CS/GCE and SS/GCE) were prepared in the same manner, respectively.

The morphologies of SS-CS/GCE and SS-CS composite were investigated by FE-SEM. It can be seen from Fig. 1A that SS-CS composite is uniformly dispersed on the surface of the GCE and a very smooth planar film is formed. As shown in Fig. 1B, SS-CS composite itself has a porous network structure. The hydrophilicity of SS-CS composite was greatly improved due to the exposed polar functional groups. Additionally, it could be used as an attractive chiral template for the recognition of Tyr enantiomers due to the chiral characteristics of the two polysaccharides.

The nitrogen (N2) adsorption-desorption isotherms were usually used for the determination of specific surface areas and pore sizes of porous materials [59–61]. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were carried out at 77 K and the specific surface areas (SBET) of SS, CS and SS-CS composite were calculated. As it can be seen in Fig. 1C, SBET of SS and CS are 1.41 and 2.34 m2 g−1, respectively, while SS-CS composite possesses increased SBET of 3.60 m2 g−1. And the average pore sizes of SS, CS and SS-CS composite were calculated to be 11.74, 14.21 and 9.81 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical properties of the modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical characterizations of the bare GCE and modified electrodes were performed by cyclic voltammetry (CV) in 10 mL of 1 mM Fe(CN)6 4-/3-. As shown in Fig. 2A, a peak-to-peak potential separation (ΔEp) of 65 mV was observed at the CS/GCE, while the ΔEp at the SS-CS/GCE, bare GCE and SS/GCE increased to 70 mV, 77 mV and 89 mV, respectively. Additionally, the peak currents at the SS-CS/GCE, bare GCE and SS/GCE decreased dramatically. The ΔEp of CS/GCE was the lowest, which can be attributed to the excellent electrocatalytic activity of CS. While the ΔEp of SS/GCE was the highest, indicating SS might hinder the electron transfer. In comparison with bare GCE and SS/GCE, SS-CS/GCE showed better electrocatalytic activity due to its porous structure, the electrocatalytic activity of CS and the synergistic effect of SS and CS, and the electron transfer was enhanced a lot at the SS-CS/GCE.

The modified electrodes were further characterized by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As we can see in Fig. 2B, the charge-transfer resistance (Rct, the high frequency intercept of the semi-circle) at bare GCE (136.2 Ω) was higher than that at SS/GCE (131.4 Ω), SS-CS/GCE (117.7 Ω) and CS/GCE (114.4 Ω). Thus, the CS/GCE has the lowest of Rct, which can be attributed to the excellent electrocatalytic activity of CS. Furthermore, the SS-CS/GCE facilitated electron-transfer reactions in the K3[Fe(CN)6]-K4[Fe(CN)6] system in comparison with bare GCE and SS/GCE, which were consistent with the results of CVs.

In order to evaluate the electrochemical properties of SS-CS/GCE, CVs of the SS-CS/GCE were performed at various scan rates (10–200 mV s−1) in 1 mM K3Fe(CN)6 solution (containing 0.1 M KCl) (Fig. 2C). As shown in Fig. 2D, the redox peak currents of SS-CS/GCE were linearly related to the square root of the scan rate in the range from 10 to 200 mV s−1, and the linear regression equations could be expressed as ipa (µA) = 2.12 ν1/2 (mV s−1) – 0.46 (R
2 = 0.9968) and ipc (µA) = -2.59 ν1/2 (mV s−1) + 1.92 (R
2 = 0.99556). The results indicated that the electrode reaction at the electrode surface might be a diffusion control process [62,63].

The electrochemical behaviors of L-Tyr on different electrodes were investigated by square wave voltammetry (SWV) in 0.1 M PB containing 1 mM L-Tyr. As shown in Fig. 2E, the oxidation peak currents of L-Tyr at CS/GCE, SS/GCE, SS-CS/GCE and bare GCE gradually increased. The current response of L-Tyr at SS/GCE was higher than that at CS/GCE, indicating that SS has better electrocatalytic activity to L-Tyr. The peak current of L-Tyr at SS-CS/GCE was higher, indicating that the synergistic effects of SS and CS were beneficial to the excellent electrocatalytic oxidation of L-Tyr.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectively chiral recognition of Tyr enantiomers
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical recognition of D-Tyr and L-Tyr was investigated by SWV. Additionally, control experiments were performed at the bare GCE, SS/GCE, CS/GCE and SS-CS/GCE. As shown in Fig. 3, the SWVs of D-Tyr and L-Tyr (1 mM, containing 0.1 M of PB, pH 7.0) at different electrodes were recorded in the potential range of 0.3–1.0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl). It can be seen that the SWVs of the bare GCE (Fig. 3A), SS/GCE (Fig. 3B) and CS/GCE (Fig. 3C) showed no differences in the peak potential, and the I L /I D ratios (peak current ratio of L -Tyr to D -Tyr) were 1.03, 0.91 and 0.99, respectively. Therefore, the bare GCE, SS/GCE and CS/GCE exhibited no recognition ability towards Tyr enantiomers.

In comparison with the peak potential difference and current response of the bare GCE, SS/GCE and CS/GCE, SS-CS/GCE (Fig. 3D) showed a relatively higher current response ratio towards L-Tyr and D-Tyr enantiomers (I L /I D = 1.38), and the peak potential difference reached 12 mV. In addition, the Ep of L-Tyr was located negatively compared to that of D-Tyr at SS-CS/GCE. The lower Ep and higher Ip of L-Tyr at SS-CS/GCE implied that SS-CS composite possessed higher affinity for L-Tyr due to the formation of H-bonds between the -OH/-NH2 groups of the SS-CS and the –COOH/-NH2 groups of L-Tyr (Scheme 1). It is presumed that only one H-bond was formed between the -NH2 group of SS-CS composite and the -COOH group of D-Tyr, resulting in lower Ip and higher Ep compared with those of L-Tyr [36,57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of amount of coated SS-CS
ce:section.ce:para: To improve the electrochemical chiral recognition of the Tyr enantiomers, the effect of amount of coated SS-CS composite was investigated. The thickness of the sensor film was determined by the amount of coated SS-CS composite, which was closely related to the electrochemical recognition efficiency of the prepared SS-CS/GCE. As we can see in Fig. 4, the peak current of L-Tyr and D-Tyr gradually increased to the maximum and then decreased when the amount of coated SS-CS gradually increased from 4 μL to 9 μL. The peak current ratio of L-Tyr to D-Tyr (I L /I D ) reached to the maximum when the amount of coated SS-CS composite was 8 μL. Thus, the optimal coating amount of SS-CS solution was set as 8 μL.

In order to stabilize the system and eliminate the capacitance, the effects of quite time were investigated [64]. The SS-CS/GCE in the test solution for some time would stabilize the coating film and thus affect its recognition efficiency for Tyr enantiomers. It can be seen in Fig. 5 that the peak current ratio of I L /I D reached the maximum when quite time was 2 s. While quite time of 5 s, 10 s, 30 s, 50 s, the I L /I D was somewhat smaller. Therefore, the optimal quite time was chosen to be 2 s.

The electrochemical behavior of SS-CS/GCE can be affected by pH, which can seriously interfere with the enantioselective detection of Tyr enantiomers. Therefore, the effects of pH of PB solution on the electrochemical behaviors of Tyr enantiomers at SS-CS/GCE were investigated. As shown in Fig. 6A, the peak currents of L -Tyr and D -Tyr at SS-CS/GCE increased continuously when the pH value increased from 5 to 7, and then decreased as the pH value increased from 7 to 9. The two natural polysaccharides, SS and CS, are unstable under strong acid and alkaline conditions [25]. The peak current ratio of I L /I D between L-Tyr and D-Tyr reached its maximum when pH value was 7. Therefore, buffer solution with pH of 7.0 was used in the following experiments.

In addition, the oxidation peak potential of L-Tyr and D-Tyr shifted to a negative potential with an increase in pH (Fig. 6B), indicating that protons are directly involved in the oxidation of Tyr at SS-CS/GCE. From the regression equation of potential and pH in Fig. 6B, it's easy to see that the slope values are close to the theoretical value (59 mV/pH), revealing that equal proton-electron transfer was involved in the electrode process.

Both intramolecular hydrogen bonds (H-bonds) and intermolecular H-bonds are significantly affected by temperature, and the chiral recognition of Tyr enantiomers by SS-CS composite may be affected by temperature. The effects of temperature on the recognition efficiency were studied in this work (
Fig. 7), and the highest recognition efficiency (IL/ID = 1.38, ΔEp = 12 mV) was obtained at 25 °C. It might be attributed to the fact that both SS and CS could form stable H-bonds with L-Tyr at 25 °C, and the synergistic effect of the SS-CS composite led to higher recognition efficiency. At temperatures below 25 °C, the intermolecular H-bonds between SS and CS were highly stable, and it is difficult to form additional H-bonds between SS-CS and L-Tyr, thereby the recognition efficiency decreased at lower temperatures. And vice versa the H-bonds formed between the SS-CS composite and L-Tyr would be broken at high temperatures (above 25 °C), which would reduce the chiral recognition efficiency of SS-CS/GCE toward Tyr enantiomers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical chiral detection of Tyr enantiomers
ce:section.ce:para:
SWV analysis of Tyr enantiomers with different concentrations at the SS-CS/GCE in 0.1 M PB solution (pH 7.0) was carried out under optimized conditions. ( Fig. 8, inset a, b). The peak currents linearly increased with an increase in the concentration of Tyr enantiomers, indicating the electrode was sensitive to quantitative determination of both D-Tyr and L-Tyr. Calibration curves (Fig. 8) for the measurements of Tyr enantiomers showed a linear relationship between the SWV peak currents and the concentrations of Tyr enantiomers. SWV behaviors of Tyr in the concentration range of 0.01–1.0 mM at the SS-CS/GCE showed two regression equations of ip (μA) = 11.80 C
L-Tyr + 0.40 (R
2 = 0.98333) and ip (μA) = 9.84C
D-Tyr + 0.28 (R
2 = 0.99631). Based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (S/N = 3), the detection limits of SS-CS/GCE for L-Tyr and D-Tyr were 0.35 μM and 0.42 μM, respectively. These results indicated that the chiral sensor exhibited good detection ability for Tyr enantiomers.

A comparison of this work with other previously reported electrochemical methods for chiral recognition of Tyr enantiomers is summarized in Table 1. Cu2-α-CD-CS/GCE, β-CDs-GQDs/GCE, L-Cys-Au-Fe3O4/MGCE, SA/GCE, AuNPs/GCE and CyA/GCE were prepared by electrodeposition methods, while polypyrrole molecularly imprinted Ni electrode was fabricated by a thermal polymerization method. However, both electrodeposition and thermal polymerization were relatively costly and time-consuming. Obviously, the developed SS-CS/GCE possessed many advantages such as easy-to-fabricate, facile, rapid and inexpensive, making it be a promising electrochemical chiral sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti‑interference, reproducibility and stability of SS-CS/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The anti-interference ability of SS-CS/GCE was tested toward the detection of 0.1 mM of Tyr enantiomers in the presence of 10-fold higher concentrations of physiological substances such as ascorbic acid (AA), glucose (Glc), D-phenylalanine (D-Phe), L-glutamic acid (L-Glu), L-lysine (L-Lys), L-threonine (L-Thr), L-cysteine (L-Cys) and some metal compounds such as potassium chloride (KCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), lead nitrate [Pd(NO3)2], nickel nitrate [Ni(NO3)2], magnesium nitrate [Mg(NO3)2], aluminum nitrate [Al(NO3)3], copper sulfate [CuSO4], ferric chloride [FeCl3], potassium dichromate [K2Cr2O7], zinc nitrate [Zn(NO3)2] and cadmium nitrate [Cd(NO3)2]. As shown in Fig. 9, the interfering species exhibited no influence on the response signals to Tyr enantiomers, and the deviations are less than 9.97%, indicating the proposed SS-CS/GCE possessed good anti-interference ability.

To evaluate the reproducibility of SS-CS/GCE, the continuous measurement of a same electrode in 0.1 mM Tyr enantiomers for six times was performed, and a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.82% for L-Tyr and 2.50% for D-Tyr could be obtained. The stability of SS-CS/GCE was also evaluated by continuously measuring a same electrode for 6 times in five consecutive working days, and the RSD of L-Tyr and D-Tyr were 6.59% and 6.98%, respectively. As shown in Fig. 10, the reusability of SS-CS/GCE was assessed by continuously measuring a same electrode in 0.05 mM L-Tyr and D-Tyr solutions, and the current deviations of 11.07% for L-Tyr and 9.96% for D-Tyr could be observed even after 7 trials, respectively. Therefore, the proposed electrode exhibited high reproducibility and stability, and relatively good reusability for the determination of Tyr enantiomers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chiral recognition of Tyr enantiomers in racemic solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of a chiral sensor for predicting the ratio of L- to D-isomer in racemic solutions is crucial for its practical application [65]. In this work, the oxide peak current responses to the mixtures of L-Tyr and D-Tyr with a total concentration of 1 mM at the proposed SS-CS/GCE were recorded. It can be seen in Fig. 11A that the two oxide peaks of L-Tyr and D-Tyr were merged into a single one when regardless of the relative amount of D-Tyr wt% in the mixture. The Ep of the oxidation peak shifted positively and the oxide peak currents decreased with an increase of D-Tyr wt%. The oxide peak currents were linear with D-Tyr wt% in mixture solutions (Fig. 11B), and the Ep was almost linear with D-Tyr wt% (Fig. 11 C). In comparison with the previously reported results [24], SS-CS/GCE possessed relatively higher recognition efficiency for each individual enantiomer in mixture solutions. The results provide some practical guidance for qualitatively and quantitatively recognition of D-Tyr wt% at SS-CS/GCE in a mixture of L- and D-Tyr enantiomers. More researches are needed to improve the chiral recognition efficiency of the developed sensor for each individual enantiomer in mixture solutions.

To summarize, a novel chiral sensor based on synergistic effect of SS-CS composite modified GCE was developed and used for electrochemical recognition of Tyr enantiomers. SWV was used to investigate the electrochemical response of Tyr enantiomers at the SS-CS/GCE, and the selective formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds between Tyr enantiomers and SS-CS contributed to the chiral recognition. The peak current ratio between L-Tyr and D-Tyr reached 1.38, and the peak potential difference (ΔEp = ED - EL) was 12 mV. In addition, the Tyr enantiomer concentrations showed a good linear relationship with the peak current values. In the concentration range of 0.01–1.0 mM, good coefficients of determination (R 2
L-Tyr = 0.98333 and R 2
D-Tyr = 0.99631). The detection limit of 0.35 μM and 0.42 μM (S/N = 3) were obtained for L-Tyr and D-Tyr at the SS-CS/GCE, respectively. More importantly, the SS-CS/GCE showed the ability to predict the ratios of L-Tyr and D-Tyr in a racemic mixture, extending the wide future applications of the natural polysaccharides-based electrochemical chiral systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51674292 and 21471163), Provincial Natural Science Foundation of Hunan (No. 2016JJ1023), Hunan Provincial Science and Technology Plan Project, China (No. 2018TP1003) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of Central South University (No. 2018zzts361).

ce:section.ce:para: None.
